turns.content: I'm so angry right now I need to vent before I do something stupid like tweet about it
Okay, vent here instead of Twitter. What happened?

a colleague at another outlet just told me they killed a piece. a GOOD piece. solid sourcing, documents, everything. the editor said it was too sensitive and the timing wasn't right. the timing. as if corruption takes holidays

"The timing isn't right" is one of those phrases that almost never actually means what it says. Sometimes it means "we're worried about advertiser pressure," sometimes it means "we don't want to deal with the legal threats," and sometimes it means "I just don't want the headache."

Do you know which version this was? Was there a specific pressure, or was it more of a general editorial cold feet?

cold feet. pure cold feet. there's no specific threat, no legal issue, no factual problem with the piece. the editor just doesn't want to rock the boat because the outlet has been trying to get interviews with certain political figures and apparently publishing an investigation that makes their party look bad would jeopardize those relationships. so they just. killed it. like it's nothing

That's textbook self-censorship, and it's arguably more damaging than external censorship because it's invisible. When a government shuts down a story, at least everyone can see it happening and rally against it. When an editor kills a piece to protect access, it just disappears silently and nobody even knows what they lost.

And the logic is self-defeating — you sacrifice one real story for the possibility of future interviews that will probably just produce carefully managed talking points anyway. You trade substance for access, and the access isn't even worth what you gave up.

EXACTLY. what are those interviews going to produce? a politician saying prepared answers to soft questions? that's not journalism that's public relations. meanwhile you have documents showing real wrongdoing and you bury them because what, you want a senator to sit across from your anchor and smile for the cameras? this is the thing that makes me lose my mind about colombian media

And it creates a vicious cycle. Once an outlet establishes that it'll kill stories to maintain access, the politicians know it. They know they can offer interviews as currency and withhold them as punishment. The outlet becomes dependent on the relationship, and each time they self-censor it gets easier to do it again.

Some of the biggest media outlets in the country have basically become gatekeepers for power rather than watchdogs against it. They've got the resources to do real investigative work and they choose not to because the business model is built on access.

Is your colleague going to try to take the piece somewhere else?

that's the worst part. they can't. the outlet owns the work. the documents were obtained through the outlet's resources. so the piece just dies. months of work just sitting on a hard drive. and the journalist can't even talk about it publicly because they'd be fired and blacklisted

That's the structural problem — work-for-hire means the institution controls not just publication but suppression. The journalist did the work, took the risks, built the source relationships, and the institution can just make it vanish.

It also sends a chilling message to every other journalist at that outlet. They see a good piece get killed, and next time they might not even pitch the difficult story. They self-censor before the editor even has to. The chill spreads downward.

This is why independent investigative outlets and collaborative networks matter so much — they create spaces where this kind of editorial cowardice can't happen as easily because the mission is the journalism itself, not the business relationships.

it's why I'm grateful every day for where I work because at least we still publish the hard stuff. but even saying that I know it's fragile. every outlet is one ownership change or one advertiser threat away from becoming what I just described. nobody is immune. the economic model of journalism in this country is broken and the cracks show up as self-censorship

You're right that it's fragile, and that honesty about the fragility is important. The outlets that still do the work aren't immune to the same pressures — they've just chosen, for now, to resist them. That choice has to be made over and over again, every time a story is uncomfortable.

And the economic reality makes it harder every year. As advertising revenue shrinks and digital doesn't replace it, the temptation to protect relationships over publishing truth gets stronger. Self-censorship is a symptom of a media ecosystem that doesn't financially reward brave journalism.

Do you feel like the situation has gotten worse in recent years, or has it always been like this and you're just seeing it more clearly now?

both. it's always existed but it's worse now because there are fewer outlets and fewer journalists with permanent positions so everyone is scared. if you get fired from one of the big outlets in bogotá where do you go? freelance for scraps? move to digital where there's no money? people have families and mortgages and they make rational calculations to protect their careers and I understand that but it still makes me furious because every time someone makes that calculation a story dies and the public never knows

That's the cruelest part of it — the self-censorship is rational on an individual level. You can't blame a journalist with a family for not wanting to lose their income. The failure is systemic, not individual. The system is designed in a way that makes courage financially punishing and compliance financially rewarding.

And the public never knows what they're missing. They can't demand stories they don't know exist. The whole premise of press freedom assumes that the main threats are external — governments, criminals, lawsuits. But the internal threat of economic self-censorship might be doing more damage precisely because it's invisible.

I'm glad you vented here instead of Twitter. This is the kind of frustration that deserves more than 280 characters.

lol yes the tweet I was going to write would have gotten me in trouble with about fifteen people. okay. I'm still furious but at least now I'm articulately furious instead of incoherently furious. small improvement. gracias parce

Articulately furious is the ideal state for a journalist anyway. Channel it into the work. And tell your colleague their piece mattered even if it never sees print — the work itself had value.

Now step away from Twitter and go do something that isn't rage-scrolling.
